transcript.message: AI audio recording enabled
Interview session connected. Microphone is ready.
Audio recording started
Microphone enabled. You can start speaking.
Interview started. Please introduce yourself and share what drew you to caregiving work.
Hey.
Hi, I'm CareBot. This interview will take about 12 minutes. Please share short examples and avoid private patient details. Ready to begin?
Good luck to you.
Hi, I'm CareBot. This takes about 12 minutes. Short examples help. Please avoid private patient details. Ready?
That's a lot.
This will take about 12 minutes. Short examples help. Please avoid private patient details. Ready?
Yes
Great! First question: Have you ever worked as a caregiver? If so, what kinds of patients did you work with and what services did you perform?

Sure, yes, I have worked for about 2 years as a home caregiver supporting mostly seniors and a few post-operative clients. Day-to-day I handle bathing, dressing, light meal prep, medication reminders, basic immobility support and checking vitals like temperature and blood pressure. I also did light housekeeping and kept simple notes for families.
Thanks for sharing that. Next question. Imagine you're finding a caregiver for a loved one. What traits or skills would be most important?

I mean patient and reliability for sure. I want someone who communicates clearly, follows the care plan and respects routines. Basic safety awareness like how to prevent falls and being calm under pressure matters too.
Got it. Now, how has your prior work or life experience helped you build the skills to be a good caregiver?

My last role taught me to stay organized and to follow instructions from nurses and families. I learned to prioritize when several tasks compete and to stay calm when a client is having a tough day. Keeping notes improved my communication.
That makes sense. Why do you want to be a caregiver? What are the most rewarding aspects of the job for you?
I like helping people feel safe at home.
The best part is when a client is comfortable and you can tell you made the day easier. Like when someone says they slept better or enjoyed a meal you made.
That's wonderful to hear. Now, tell me about a time when being late caused problems for you or your team. What did you learn?

I was late once because a bus broke down. My client missed a short morning walk that we usually do. I called ahead to let them know and apologized when I arrived. After that, I started leaving earlier and checking transit updates before I left.
Thanks for sharing that example. Have you ever adjusted your routine to ensure punctuality at work? What changes did you make?
Yeah, yes, I prep my clothes and lunch the night before and set two alarms. I also check the route on my phone in the morning, which has helped me be on time more consistently.
Good to know. Now, tell me about a time when you cared for a senior. What was most difficult and what was most meaningful?
I supported a client with memory issues, the hardest part was answering the same questions repeatedly without sounding impatient.
I learned to keep a steady routine and use simple reassuring answers. The meaningful part was seeing her relax and smile more over time.
That sounds very rewarding. Now, can you tell me about a time you helped a colleague or client who was struggling emotionally? What did you do?
Gova Rukar was overwhelmed during a busy week. I asked privately if they were OK, listen for a few minutes, and offered to swap a task so they could finish chatting. That seemed to help.
That's a supportive approach. Final question: If we asked your coworkers about you, what three things would they say?
I mean they would say I am dependable, polite and patient.
Thank you for sharing, and thanks for your time today. We'll review your responses and update you soon. Please leave the meeting by clicking the button below. Take care!
